1: Standard oracle-reception formula. Chapter 21 opens a sustained sword-oracle that constitutes Ezekiel's most concentrated meditation on the divine instrument of judgment. The Hebrew chapter division places the pivot differently: Matthew 21:1 = English Ezekiel 20:45, making the 'south/forest' oracle the opening of this sword sequence. The English chapter numeration follows the LXX.
2: sim panecha derekh teman ('set your face toward the south'): sim panim (set the face) is Ezekiel's characteristic direction-formula for prophetic address — the prophet physically orients toward the target of the oracle. The 'forest' of the Negev is a figurative description, likely of Judah, since the Negev is not forested. The oracle establishes the direction before the target is named.
3: ya'ar haNegev ('forest of the south/Negev'): the allegory thin — the south-forest is Jerusalem-Judah. Fire devoured (achel) in the forest prepares for the explicit announcement that follows: 'I am about to draw my sword' (Ezekiel 21:4). Every tree — righteous and wicked — is consumed, establishing the totalistic nature of the coming judgment.
4: tzaddiq veresha ('righteous and wicked'): the sword indiscriminately cuts off both — a theological difficulty the exiles press (Ezekiel 18:1-4; Ezekiel 33:10-20). YHWH's answer throughout chapters 18 and 33 is individual accountability, but here the sword oracle emphasizes the totalistic corporate judgment on the land as a whole. Both registers must be held: corporate judgment and individual accountability operate at different levels.
5: kol-basar yeda ki-ani YHWH heretzti et-charvi mita'rea ('all flesh will know that I, YHWH, have drawn my sword from its sheath'): the universal-recognition formula (yeda', they will know) applied to the sword-drawing. The sword in the sheath is a standard ancient Near Eastern idiom for restrained military power; drawing the sword means the restraint is now removed. YHWH himself is the one who draws — not merely permitting Babylonian aggression but actively initiating it.
6: ne'aneq beshivron motnayim ('sigh with the breaking/shattering of the loins'): the root n'q (to sigh, groan, moan) combined with shivron motnayim (collapsing/breaking of loins — the body's core giving way). Ezekiel is commanded to perform grief so physically intense that it signals structural collapse — not mere sadness but bodily breakdown. The sign-act makes the prophet's body the first casualty of the announced judgment.
7: lema ah atah ne'aneq ('why are you sighing?'): the audience query that triggers the public explanation — the sign-act requires interrogation to become proclamation. The answer frames the coming catastrophe as something that will make 'every heart melt, all hands hang limp, every spirit faint, every knee turn to water' — the full physiological register of paralytic terror (cf. Ezekiel 7:17).
8: Transition to the formal sword poem/oracle. The second oracle within the chapter sequence marks an escalation: from sign-act (sighing) to direct poetic speech about the sword. The sword oracle in Ezekiel 21:8-17 is recognized as among the most intense martial poetry in the Hebrew Bible.
9: cherev cherev huchaddah vegam meruqah ('a sword, a sword — sharpened and polished'): the doubled cherev intensifies urgency (cf. Jeremiah 4:10 and other doubled words of alarm). Huchaddah (sharpened, Hophal of chadd) and meruqah (polished, Pual of maraq): the sword is combat-ready — the double preparation suggests both effectiveness (sharpened) and terror (gleaming). The war-readiness of the divine instrument is total.
10: lematbeach tivchah ('sharpened to make a slaughter'): the sword's purpose is stated baldly — slaughter (tevach). The second half of the verse is notoriously difficult: o nasis shevet beni — 'or shall we rejoice/dismiss the rod of my son?' may be a shocked question rejecting comfort-seeking when the scepter of Israel has itself become the target. The shevet (rod/scepter) that should represent Davidic authority is now itself being swept away.
11: vetitten ota limrut lamatbeach lachuf ('it is given to be polished, to be handled, to make a slaughter'): three infinitives of purpose — the sword exists to be polished, handled, and used for slaughter. The passive 'it is given' (nittenah) signals divine agency without explicitly naming YHWH as the giver. The sword has been commissioned and prepared; all that remains is its deployment.
12: za'aq vehaveil ('cry out and wail'): direct commands to Ezekiel to perform lamentation. hak-yerekha ('strike your thigh'): a gesture of distress — literally striking the upper thigh as a sign of horror and grief. Compare Jeremiah 31:19 where the same gesture marks repentance. Ki-hi haytah vichal-nesi'ei Yisrael ('for it will be against all the princes of Israel') — the royal class is named as the sword's target.
13: bachan ('test/proof'): a brief, cryptic verse — 'it is tested' or 'because it is a test.' The verb bachan (to test, examine, prove) suggests the sword-judgment is also a divine probation — YHWH's action will prove the truth of his word and the nature of Israel's condition. Vema tihyeh im-gam mateh bozit ('what then if even the despising rod will be no more?') — the scepter of authority has been judged useless.
14: hak-yad el-yad ('clap hand to hand'): Ezekiel is commanded to clap hands together — a sign of either grief or bitter irony (cf. Ezekiel 6:11; Ezekiel 22:13). Cherev shalishit ('the sword doubled/tripled'): the sword is to strike again and again — doubled, then tripled — against the slain. The intensification through repetition drives home the completeness of the judgment.
15: lema'an lamug lev ('in order that hearts may melt'): the sword's psychological as well as physical purpose — not merely killing but creating the paralytic terror in all who witness. Every gate is targeted (achad sha'ar mamgurut cherev''am — the sword positioned at every gate), ensuring there is no exit. The sword threatens from every direction.
16: hitchaddi yemini hasmili le'anaph panayich ('be sharp, go right; direct yourself, go left — wherever your face is directed'): the command to the sword to be versatile, omnidirectional. The sword's freedom of movement in any direction mirrors the chariot's omnidirectional movement from chapter 1 — both are divine instruments of perfect responsiveness to the divine will.
17: vegam ani acha el-kappi ('and I also will clap my hands'): YHWH himself joins the clapping — the divine gesture that Ezekiel performed (Ezekiel 21:14) is now performed by YHWH. vahinnichoti chemati ('and I will satisfy/rest my fury'): nuach (Hiphil, 'to cause to rest') — the fury that has been building is brought to completion. The word nuach is also the word for 'Noah' (Noach), though the pun is not in view here. The fury rests when it is fully enacted.
18: New oracle: sim lecha shetei derakhim ('set for yourself two ways/roads'): the road-sign oracle. Ezekiel is to draw a map showing two roads from Babylon — one to Jerusalem, one to Rabbah of Ammon. The dual-target establishes that Babylon's judgment is not specific to Judah but applies to the whole region. The 'sword of the king of Babylon' serves YHWH's purposes in both directions.
19: veber'i bara' ('and engrave an engraving'): the carved road-marker at the crossroads points both ways. Rosh derekh ir ('the head of the road to a city') — the fork in the road. The military intelligence Babylon actually used (divination at crossroads) is described here from the perspective of YHWH's sovereign direction of Babylonian decision-making: their divination will happen to point toward Jerusalem because YHWH has determined that outcome.
20: lesim qesem qesem cherev el-Yerushalayim velo el-Rabbah Bnei-Ammon ('to set divination — the sword against Jerusalem, and against Rabbah of Ammon'): two targets of the same Babylonian sword. The oracle holds both in view simultaneously — Israel is not specially targeted; rather, the sword reaches all who stand under divine judgment. Ammon will receive its oracle in Ezekiel 21:28-32.
21: ki-amad melech Bavel el-em haderekh bereshit shetei hadderakhim liqsom qasem ('for the king of Babylon stands at the fork of the two roads, at the head of the two ways, to use divination'): qesem qesamim (divination/divination) — an intensive or generalized form. Three divination methods listed: herak bacheitzim (shaking arrows/belomancy), consulting teraphim (shal baterafim), and examining a liver (ra'ah bakabed). All three are condemned in Deuteronomy, yet here YHWH uses even Babylonian divination to accomplish his purpose.
22: beyad-yemino hayah haqesem Yerushalayim ('in his right hand was the divination — Jerusalem'): the lot falls on Jerusalem. Whatever the Babylonian diviner intended, the outcome was divinely predetermined. Latum krim (to direct battering rams), lif'toch peh beretach (to open the mouth in slaughter/with a shout), leharim qol biteruah (to raise the voice with a battle cry) — the military operations described in technical detail: siege equipment, shouting, and war-cry.
23: uhem kiqsam shav be'eineihem ('and it will be to them like a false/empty divination in their eyes'): the inhabitants of Jerusalem will dismiss the Babylonian divination as meaningless — their false prophets have told them they are safe. The sworn oaths (shevuot shavu'ot) with which they have been reassured will create a false confidence that makes the judgment more devastating when it falls. The same dynamic recurs in Ezekiel 13:10: 'peace when there is no peace.'
24: laken koh amar Adonai YHWH ya'an hizkarekhem avonekhem ('therefore, because you have caused your iniquity to be remembered'): the Babylonian divination that Jerusalem dismisses as empty is precisely the means by which YHWH 'remembers' Israel's iniquity — that is, brings it into the sphere of active judgment. Avon (iniquity) remembered by YHWH is iniquity being brought to account.
25: ve'atah chalal rasha nesi Yisrael ('and you, O profaned wicked prince of Israel'): direct address to Zedekiah (or the Davidic monarchy generally). Chalal (profaned/pierced) — the prince has been desacralized, stripped of the protective holiness of the anointed office. Asher-ba yomo be'et avon qetz ('whose day has come, at the time when iniquity reaches its end') — the convergence of the prince's failure and the end of the age of iniquity.
26: haser hamitznefet veharim haketer ('remove the turban and lift off the crown'): two objects removed. Mitznefet (turban) is the high priestly turban (Ex Ezekiel 28:4; Ezekiel 39:28) — here worn by the prince, blurring royal and priestly roles. Keter (crown) is the royal diadem. The removal of both signals the simultaneous dissolution of both offices. Zot lo-zot ('this shall not be this'): the divine declaration that the present order of things is reversed — the high brought low and the low raised.
27: avvah avvah avvah asimennah ('overturned, overturned, overturned — I will make it'): the triple repetition of avvah (ruin/overturning) is unparalleled in Hebrew prose — three successive destructions of the Davidic throne (Jehoahaz, Jehoiakim, Zedekiah — each deported or killed). gam-zot lo haytah ('also this shall not be') — the throne ceases. Then: ad-bo asher-lo hamishpat venatattihu ('until he comes to whom the right/judgment belongs, and to him I will give it'): the suspension formula — the throne is overturned and held in eschatological suspension until the one to whom it belongs by right arrives. This echoes Genesis 49:10 (ad ki-yavo Shiloh) — the scepter withheld until the one to whom it belongs claims it.
28: uven-adam hinave el-Bnei Ammon ('and son of man, prophesy to the Ammonites'): the sword that was directed toward Jerusalem now pivots to Ammon. The oracle shows that YHWH's judgment is not partialistic: all nations under divine indictment receive the sword. Ammon mocked Israel's downfall (Ezekiel 25:3-7); their turn is coming. The Ammonite oracle is brief compared to the Jerusalem oracle — the sustained focus on Israel reflects the depth of the covenant relationship.
29: cherev cherev petuyah latatbeach meruqah ('a sword, a sword unsheathed for slaughter, polished'): the sword poem that opened in Ezekiel 21:9 is reprised for Ammon — the same sharpened, polished sword turns toward Ammon. Chazot shav ('while they see false visions') — the Ammonite prophets who offered false comfort will be proved wrong as surely as Israel's false prophets were proved wrong. The sword does not spare those whose prophets promised peace.
30: hashev el-ta'areh ('return it to its sheath'): a shift — the command to return the sword to its sheath. This is not the end of judgment but a temporary withdrawal from the Ammonite oracle, anticipating the fuller judgment oracle against Ammon in chapter 25. Bimqom asher nivrate sham tishafet ('in the place where you were created, there you will be judged') — the land of origin is also the land of judgment.
31: shafachti alecha za'mi ('I will pour out my indignation upon you'): za'am (indignation, burning anger) — a term used for YHWH's most intense categorical anger. Nishaft'ti alecha be'esh evrati ('I will blow on you with the fire of my wrath') — the breath/blowing of divine wrath as fire. The same terms used for judgment on Jerusalem (Ezekiel 7:8; Ezekiel 22:22) are applied to Ammon.
32: uchol-Yerushalayim umikol-ha'aretz ('to all Jerusalem and all the land'): the Ammonite oracle ends with the declaration that Ammon will be 'fuel for the fire' — burned as fuel rather than smelted as metal (the distinction between destruction and refining). Lo tizakher ki ani YHWH dibarti ('you will not be remembered, for I, YHWH, have spoken'): the finality formula — Ammon will be forgotten, not preserved as a witness. The covenant nation Israel receives judgment but is preserved through it; Ammon receives judgment and is obliterated. The distinction between covenant and non-covenant judgment is stark.
